data.title: Axfood AB (publ) gives FY results
data.description:
• Axfood AB (publ) press release (AXFOF): FY Non-GAAP EPS of SEK2.60.
  • Revenue of SEK22.83B (+4.4% Y/Y).
  • Adjusted operating profit amounted to SEK 873 m (772) and the adjusted operating margin to 3.8% (3.5).
  • Retail sales increased 8.7% to SEK 20,468 m (18,836). Excl. City Gross, retail sales increased 5.3%.
  • Operating profit amounted to SEK 860 m (629) including items affecting comparability of SEK -13 m (-143). The operating margin was 3.8% (2.9).

data.title: Euro Area economic sentiment rises in January
data.description:
• The Economic Optimism Index in the Euro Area increased to 99.40 points in January from 97.20 points in December of 2025.
  • The Consumer Confidence increased to -12.40 points in January from -13.20 points in December.
  • The Consumer Confidence Price Trends decreased to 24.10 points in January from 26.70 points in December.

data.title: SAP's stock falls after cloud backlog miss
data.description:
Shares of SAP (SAP) fell about 13% premarket on Thursday after current cloud backlog in the fourth quarter came in below expectations.

In the fourth quarter, the current cloud backlog grew by 16% to €21.05B and was up 25% at constant currencies. However, CEO Christian Klein had earlier said that SAP was targeting 26% growth, Bloomberg News reported.

The current cloud backlog in the fourth quarter missed SAP’s own target. “If you look at the pipeline, and I’ve seen it many times, I would rather see 25% as a disappointment,” said Klein on the company's third quarter earnings call in October 2025. “26% would be, in my eyes, a great result.”

Klein said in a call with analysts on Thursday that the lower current cloud backlog was partly due to negotiations taking longer. "It’s a reflection of what is happening in the world,” Klein said, adding that clients are asking about potential sanctions, export restrictions, and new regulations. “This is not a reflection of a demand issue,” according to the report.

For the fourth quarter ended Dec. 31, non-IFRS EPS (basic) grew 16% year-over-year to €1.62. Total revenue rose 3% (9% at constant currencies) year-over-year to €9.68.

SAP said Cloud revenue rose 19% to €5.61B and up 26% at constant currencies. Cloud ERP Suite revenue grew 23% to €4.86B and up 30% at constant currencies.

"Q4 was a strong cloud quarter, with bookings resulting in 30% Total Cloud Backlog growth to a record 77 billion Euros. The significant Current Cloud Backlog growth in Q4 has laid a strong foundation for accelerating Total Revenue growth through 2027. SAP Business AI has become a main driver for growth as it was included in two thirds of our Q4 cloud order entry, combined with strong AI adoption across the ERP Suite," said Klein in the company's earnings release on Thursday.

The company noted that as of Dec. 31, 2025, total cloud backlog was up 22% to €77.29B and up 30% at constant currencies.

Cloud revenue for the full year 2025 rose 23% to €21.02B and was up 26% at constant currencies.

Klein said on a call with analysts that the threat to SAP’s business from AI is overstated, as large language models, or LLMs, alone cannot be depended on for some use cases and that the underlying business data SAP has access to is necessary. “To make this very clear, we are winning deals because of AI. We are not losing deals because of AI,” Klein noted, the report added.

Outlook

For 2026, SAP expects €25.8B to €26.2B cloud revenue at constant currencies (2025: €21.02B), up 23% to 25% at constant currencies. This compares to an average analyst estimate of €26B, according to Bloomberg.

SAP noted that constant currency current cloud backlog growth will slightly decelerate in 2026 (2025: 25%).

The company expects constant currency total revenue growth to accelerate through 2027.

Buyback

SAP announced a new share repurchase program worth up to €10B. It is scheduled to start in February and is expected to be completed by the end of 2027.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: MSFT, META, TSLA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were trading higher in the premarket hours of Thursday as market participants digested the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision and a slate of mega-cap tech earnings.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • International Business Machines (IBM) +8% - Shares jumped after the company posted Q4 results and 2026 guidance that topped expectations. Free cash flow rose 23% YoY to $7.55B, beating estimates, while IBM said its generative AI book of business now exceeds $12.5B. Software delivered double-digit growth, and Infrastructure also posted double-digit gains driven by strong adoption of the next-generation mainframe. Looking ahead, IBM guided to over 5% constant-currency revenue growth in 2026 and about $1B in incremental free cash flow, above Street forecasts.
  • Meta Platforms (META) +7% - Shares surged after the company delivered a strong Q4 with top-line growth of 24% Y/Y and issued an upbeat Q1 revenue outlook of $53.5B–$56.5B vs. a consensus of $51.4B. Operating income increased 6% despite margins compressing as costs surged, driven by heavy AI and infrastructure investment. Meta lifted its 2026 capex outlook to $115B–$135B but said operating income in 2026 should still exceed 2025 levels, easing concerns around spending discipline.
  • Tesla (TSLA) +3% - Shares rose following a Q4 top and bottom-line beat and optimistic commentary on Cybercab, robotaxi, and robotics overshadowing a sequential revenue dip. A sharp decline in automotive sales was partly offset by a 25% jump in energy generation and storage, driven by Megapack. Automotive gross margins improved sequentially, even excluding regulatory credits. Management also highlighted progress on Optimus, with the Gen 3 version expected to be unveiled in Q1 with major upgrades.

Biggest stock losers

  • Las Vegas Sands (LVS) -10% - Shares plunged despite a Q4 revenue and EPS beat, as investors reacted to a sharp miss in Macao EBITDA margins, which declined 390 bps. While Singapore delivered record results, sentiment was weighed down by structural weakness in Macao, where margins remain under pressure amid intense competition in premium segments. Management said it is refining strategy and targeting margin recovery in 2026 while reaffirming its commitment to share buybacks, long-term asset investment, and operational discipline.
  • Microsoft (MSFT) -6% - Shares slid despite beating estimates in FQ2, as investors focused on slowing cloud growth. The company earned $4.14 per share as revenue rose 17% Y/Y to $81.27B. Intelligent Cloud revenue came in at $32.91B, with Azure growth easing to 39% from 40% last quarter. Total cloud revenue was $51.5B, while Productivity and Business Processes beat expectations, but More Personal Computing slightly missed, adding to the cautious reaction.
  • ServiceNow (NOW) -5% - Shares slipped despite a strong quarter and upbeat guidance. The company forecast Q1 subscription revenue of $3.65B–$3.66B, above expectations, and full-year revenue of $15.53B–$15.57B, also ahead of consensus. ServiceNow approved a $5B buyback and is launching a $2B accelerated repurchase. It also expanded its partnership with Anthropic to embed Claude models deeper into its AI platform.

data.title: Nasdaq futures rise as Tesla, Meta earnings cheer investors
data.description:
Stock index futures pointed higher on Thursday as investors digested a wave of tech earnings.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.42% to 7,007.06. Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.60% to 26,177.93, while Dow futures (INDU) +0.27% to 49,149.63.

Tesla (TSLA) shares were up 2.9% in premarket trade as a top- and bottom-line beat was coupled with an upbeat outlook on Cybercab and robotaxi growth. Meta Platforms (META) gained more than 7% after posting its fourth-quarter earnings, where it beat Wall Street expectations for revenues and profitability.

Microsoft (MSFT) shares fell more than 5% even as the tech giant reported fiscal second-quarter results that topped estimates.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve also kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75%

Treasury yields edged higher across the curve. The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) ticked up to 3.58%, adding 0.17%. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved higher to 4.27%, rising 0.45%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) increased to 4.88%, up 0.70%.

Top gainers in premarket trading included Meta Platforms (META) +7.96%, International Business Machines (IBM) +7.87%, and C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW) +6.19%. Decliners included Las Vegas Sands (LVS) -9.89%, Microsoft (MSFT) -5.77%, and ServiceNow (NOW) -5.28%.
